observations_from_site: Detected 6 no's of tube leakage at SSH Zone

why_steps.question: Why 1
why_steps.answer: May be due to Low steam flow

why_steps.question: Why 2
why_steps.answer: But as per the record data inspections its found that the all operating parameters are within the acceptable range

why_steps.question: Why 3
why_steps.answer: May be due to the steam pressure high

why_steps.question: Why 4
why_steps.answer: But as per the inspections pressure parameters within the operating range

why_steps.question: Why 5
why_steps.answer: Manufacturing defect indentify through PMI testing . One tube was found to be made of carbon steel instead of T-91

root_cause: Defective boiler tube material. (Material found plain carbon steel in place of T-91).

capa.action: All leakage arrested. ( Replaced with new T-91 tube spool piece )

proof_images_description: REFER PICTURE-1, REFER PICTURE-2, REFER PICTURE-3

problem_statement: GENERATOR CB TRIP DUE TO UNDER FREQUENCY.
chronology_of_events: 1450KVA DG TAKEN IN LINE DUE TO GRID POWER FAIL.
observations_from_site: 132KV GRID CB TRIP DUE TO DF/DT, THEN 42MW GEN CB TRIP DUE TO DF/DT,& THAT LOAD TRANSFER TO 15MW TG, DUE TO UNDER FREQUENCY.

why_steps.question: Why did Generator CB trip
why_steps.answer: Due to under frequency.

why_steps.question: Why was there Under Frequency
why_steps.answer: Because load transfer from 42MW TG to 15MW TG.

why_steps.question: Why did the load transfer to 15MW TG
why_steps.answer: Because 42MW TG trip due to df/dt.

why_steps.question: Why did 42MW TG trip due to df/dt
why_steps.answer: Because 132kV Grid trip due to df/dt.

capa.action: Stabilize system â€“ Restore frequency by gradual load balancing between 42 MW TG and 15 MW TG.
Collect and analyze event data â€“ Retrieve relay event logs, SCADA records, df/dt traces, and governor responses.
Inspect equipment â€“ Check Generator CB, turbine, and associated switchgear for faults or damage.
Verify relay settings â€“ Check and correct under-frequency and df/dt protection settings.
Coordinate with grid operator â€“ Obtain 132 kV grid disturbance report and verify coordination of df/dt settings.
Recommission with validated settings â€“ Test protection coordination before resynchronization.

equipment: 11 KV HT panel breaker at PCC room (Feeders No.4, 01G HT cable to Pellet 1)
problem_statement: Flash over in 11 KV HT panel breaker at PCC room
chronology_of_events: Flash over took place because of short circuit in the incomer side.
observations_from_site: Mouse got trapped between the live phases.
HT cable entry to panel was not sealed.

why_steps.question: Why did flash over take place?
why_steps.answer: Flash over took place due to short circuit.

why_steps.question: Why did short circuit occur?
why_steps.answer: Mouse got trapped between the live phases.

why_steps.question: Why did mouse get trapped?
why_steps.answer: HT cable entry to panel was not sealed.

root_cause: Improper sealing of cable entry points in HT panel.

capa.action: Cable gland to be fixed with proper sealing. to be done with complete sealing (with gland plate) at cable entry cutout point
All rest of panels to be jointly inspected and a report to be prepared.
All the defects of the inspection report to be liquidated.

problem_statement: Turbine Generator (42 MW) tripped due to high hot well level.
chronology_of_events: TG tripped on high condensate hot well level.
CEP#1 stopped, CEP#2 taken in operation.
observations_from_site: CEPs handling condensate operating with high differential pressure across strainer is surprising because condensate is supposed to be very clean.
Frequency of strainer cleaning is roughly every fortnight, even with condensate which is ultrapure.
CEP motor current data was not found stored in the DCS.
High differential pressure across the CEP strainer is surprising.
Much sediments is not found on the strainer during cleaning.

why_steps.question: Why
why_steps.answer: Turbine tripped on high hot well level.

why_steps.question: Why 1
why_steps.answer: Condensate pump could not pump out condensate from hot well.

why_steps.question: Why 2
why_steps.answer: Strainers of both condensate pumps found operating with high differential pressure.

root_cause: Manual maloperation of CEP valve by the operator (closing CEP#2 valve instead of CEP#1 valve).
breakdown_history_6months: Refer link on page-5 for strainer cleaning history.

domain_expert_insights.mechanical: 42 MW Siemens make turbine-generator unit receives steam from boiler having rating of 73 tph & 110 bar. This turbine has two vertically mounted Condensate Extraction Pumps namely CEP#1 & CEP#2 of KSB make and model WKT 100/2. These pumps extract condensate from the hotwell located below the condenser which receive saturated steam after expansion in the turbine. This collected condensate is then pumped to the deaerator after getting preheated while passing through the feed heating system. One of the two CEPs is configured to operate at a time and the other remains as stand by. For these pumps available NPSH (net positive suction head ) is very low. So these are specially designed pumps to operate under such conditions. It may noted that in these pumps the pressure drop in suction pipeline including the strainer located before the pump is designed to be minimal. For the same reason, the pump suction pipeline size is 250 NB and it is kept relatively much larger than the discharge pipeline, which is 150NB. It is important to maintain certain level in the hot well all the time. Condensate main control Valve (150NB) takes condensate out of the hotwell to the deaerator through the feed heating system and the 80 NB bypass control valve feeds condensate back to the hot well. Both these control valves are pneumatically controlled. The logic under which automatic hotwell level controller works is that with high hot well level main valve opens and bypass valve closes as per the controller output (6-20mA) and vice versa. To prevent broken turbine blades or pipeline scale etc to enter inside the pumps a bucket type strainer with 3-4 mm openings is strategically located in the suction pipeline just before these pumps. An additional fine wire mesh is wrapped over this bucket strainer, during commissioning stages as a precautionary measure to eliminate the possibilities of weld beads and welding electrode end pieces enter the pump. Over a period of time the condensate system gets fully flushed out and thereafter this additional fine mesh placed over the strainer basket is removed, as a practice,but in our case, it is still retained in position.
domain_expert_insights.electrical: CEP motor current data was not found stored in the DCS. This could have made the analysis easier.
domain_expert_insights.process: The root cause of tripping turbine reveals to be different. It can be seen from the trend that at 23:00 hours CEP#1 was stopped and then CEP#2 was taken in operation. Everything was fine and the hot well level was steadily maintained. Plan was to clean strainer of CEP#1, which had to be isolated. But due to mistake, a maloperation was done and someone closed the valve of CEP#2 in operation at 00:04 hours as evident from the sharp drop in strainer dp to zero. There had been steady rise in hot well level due to that and at 00:06 turbine trips due to high hotwell level. At 00:08, isolating valve of CEP#2 is then opened and the hot well level is normalised. During this period and beyond the status of pump#2 in DCS is showing "RUNNING". This confirms that the strainer of CEP#2 is not cleaned as claimed by power plant. The turbine was put back in operation at 00:44 hours. THIS TURBINE TRIPPING IS SIMPLY DUE TO MANUAL MALOPERATION OF CEP VALVE BY THE OPERATOR. THE OPERATOR WAS SUPPOSED TO CLOSE THE VALVE OF CEP#1 STOPPED EARLIER FOR STRAINER CLEANING BUT BY MISTAKE HE CLOSED CEP#2 VALVE LEADING TO TRIPPING OF TURBINE. It may be noted that the report of CPP-1 stating that strainer of CEP#2 was cleaned during this period is just to cover up the fact of maloperation of CEP#2 valve by the operator. The status of this pump was 'RUNNING' in DCS during this period and strainer cleaning is not possible without stopping the pump. Further, history data of strainer cleaning since the commissioning of turbine was scanned. It is surprising to find that the frequency of strainer cleaning is roughly every fortnight, even with condensate which is ultrapure. It is too high even after 5 months of machine commissioning and hence the reasons of so frequent need of strainer cleaning requires to be found out. Such a high differential pressure across the CEP strainer is surprising. What is more surprising is the fact as informed by CPP1 group is that much sediments is not found on the strainer during cleaning.

man: Operator maloperation (closed CEP#2 valve instead of CEP#1)

chronology_of_events: Whole plant load throw due to under voltage.
Phase to phase short circuit in Furnace-A of SMS -1.
Hole present in breaker allowed rat entry.
observations_from_site: hole in the Furnace-1 outgoing breaker panel

why_steps.question: Why did the incident occur?
why_steps.answer: Due to over frequency & over voltage

why_steps.question: Why over frequency & over voltage?
why_steps.answer: Whole plant load throw due to under voltage

why_steps.question: Why whole plant load throw due to under voltage?
why_steps.answer: Due to phase to phase short circuit in Furnace-A of SMS -1

why_steps.question: Why phase to phase short circuit?
why_steps.answer: Due to one rat enter inside the Furnace-1 outgoing breaker

why_steps.question: Why rat entered?
why_steps.answer: Due to one hole was present in breaker

root_cause: Due to a hole in the Furnace-1 outgoing breaker panel, a rat entered the breaker compartment and caused a phase-to-phase short circuit. This fault resulted in the tripping of the 15MW CPP due to whole plant load throw for Under voltage leading to Over frequency of the TG.

capa.action: The breaker panel hole through which the rat entered was sealed properly by using metal plate and foam spray.
All electrical panels and switchgear rooms were inspected for openings, gaps, or damaged seals, and necessary sealing was carried out.
* Consumer breaker must be provided with CBCT along with sensitive E/F. * SMS furnace breaker will be provided with voltage protection

proof_images_description: Two trend graphs are attached. One shows Frequency, the other shows Voltage. The Voltage graph indicates a maximum of 11,642V at 03:25:08.

why_steps.question: Why did the incident occur?
why_steps.answer: Due to over frequency & over voltage

why_steps.question: Why over frequency & over voltage?
why_steps.answer: Whole plant load throw due to under voltage

why_steps.question: Why whole plant load throw due to under voltage?
why_steps.answer: Due to phase to phase short circuit in Pellet-1 emergency breaker of SMS -1

why_steps.question: Why phase to phase short circuit?
why_steps.answer: Due to moisture ingress inside the Pellet-1 emergency breaker

why_steps.question: Why moisture ingress?
why_steps.answer: Due to multiple hole were present in VCB panel

root_cause: The complete plant load throw occurred due to under-voltage caused by a phase-to-phase short circuit in SMS-1. The short circuit was initiated by moisture ingress inside the Pellet-1 emergency breaker, along with the presence of multiple unsealed holes in the VCB panel. These openings allowed external contaminants, such as moisture and rodents, to enter the panel. As a result, electrical faults occurred, leading to tripping of the auxiliary power supply on under-voltage subsequent plant blackout.

capa.action: The breaker panel hole through which the mouse entered was sealed properly by using metal plate and foam spray.
All electrical panels and switchgear rooms were inspected for openings, gaps, or damaged seals, and necessary sealing was carried out.
Consumer breaker must be provided with CBCT along with sensitive E/F. SMS furnace breaker will be provided with voltage protection

proof_images_description: Generator Protection Relay (GPR) MiCOM P40 Agile panel showing 'O/F 2nd STAGE' highlighted. Panel with multiple indicator lights, including 'GENERATOR PROTECTION RELAY FAULTY (P343)', 'TURBINE LOCKOUT RELAY TRIP OIL FAULTY', 'GEN. LOCKOUT RELAY TRIP COIL FAULTY', 'GRID LOCK OUT RELAY TRIP COIL FAULTY', 'ROTOR E/F STAGE-1 PROTECTION OPTD.' illuminated.

equipment: FD fan suction damper and AFBC
problem_statement: FD fan suction damper closed and AFBC tripped

why_steps.question: 1st Why
why_steps.answer: Latest PLC logic backup was not functioning

why_steps.question: 2nd Why
why_steps.answer: Logic was mistakenly downloaded while SOV power was connected

capa.action: Ensure SOV power is disconnected before logic download, Train Engineers for download process when plant in running condition

problem_statement: The screen motor in CHP tripped because of overload.
chronology_of_events: CHP Screen motor tripped due to overload
observations_from_site: Maintenance team checked the motor and found it was not restarting.

why_steps.question: Why was there Over Current
why_steps.answer: Because of bearing failure

why_steps.question: Why did the bearing fail
why_steps.answer: Dust ingress into the bearing

why_steps.question: Why did dust enter the bearing
why_steps.answer: Defective sealing allowed dust ingress

capa.action: Fix sealing and visual inspection of motor housing regularly.
Sealing inspection and cleaning protocol added to check list.
Motor overhauling in each 6Month
